特拉华州威尔明顿。-据NBC新闻报道，乔·拜登(Joe Biden)在赢得关键的宾夕法尼亚州后，于周六成为当选总统。
据美国全国广播公司新闻报道，拜登赢得宾夕法尼亚州的20张选举人票和内华达州的6张选举人票后，获得了279张选举人票，超过了入主白宫并击败唐纳德·特朗普总统所需的270张选票。
美国全国广播公司新闻频道(NBC News)宣布，拜登的竞选将于上午11点24分在宾夕法尼亚州举行。美国东部时间，使拜登的选举人团选票总数达到273张，并允许网络为他举行选举。下午2点过后不久。美国东部时间，NBC新闻呼吁在内华达州为拜登举行竞选。
拜登的胜利为现代史上持续时间最长、最动荡的竞选画上了句号。在这场竞选中，他一直积极关注特朗普处理新冠肺炎疫情广受批评的做法。根据NBC新闻出口民调对提前和选举日选民的初步结果，大多数选民表示，冠状病毒病例数量的上升是他们投票的一个重要因素。
拜登经常批评特朗普不适合担任总统，并将他的竞选定位为一场争夺美国灵魂的战争。他从竞选之初就承诺，如果他获胜，将治愈和团结这个国家。他在闭幕词中的核心承诺是，既代表那些投票给他的人，也代表那些在他入主白宫时没有投票给他的人。
在美国全国广播公司新闻频道(NBC News)宣布竞选后不久发表的一份声明中，拜登表示，他对这一消息感到荣幸，并重申了近几周来他竞选演讲中的一个特点--团结的呼吁。
拜登说：我对美国人民对我和当选副总统哈里斯的信任感到荣幸和谦卑。
随着竞选活动的结束，是时候把愤怒和刺耳的言辞抛在脑后，团结成一个国家了。现在是美国团结起来的时候了。还有疗伤，他说。我们是美利坚合众国。如果我们齐心协力，没有什么是我们做不到的。
拜登将于周六晚上8点向全国发表讲话。来自威尔明顿的ET，他的竞选团队说。
他的竞选伙伴、加利福尼亚州参议员卡玛拉·哈里斯(Kamala Harris)在推特上表示，这次选举的意义远不止拜登和她自己。它关乎美国的灵魂和我们为之奋斗的意愿。我们还有很多工作要做。让我们开始吧，她说。随后的一条推文显示了一段视频，视频中她打电话给拜登，动情地告诉他：乔，我们做到了。你将成为下一任美国总统。
比赛结束后不久，全国各地就爆发了庆祝活动和抗议活动。拜登的支持者很快聚集在纽约的时代广场和大军广场，华盛顿特区白宫附近的黑人生命也是广场，以及费城宾夕法尼亚会议中心外。
支持特朗普的集会也在许多地方爆发，抗议拜登获胜，包括密歇根州议会大厦外的兰辛，以及北卡罗来纳州议会大厦外的罗利。
拜登将于1月20日宣誓就任第46任总统。哈里斯将成为第一位女性、第一位黑人和第一位南亚裔美国副总统。
作为总统，拜登将立即面对一个严重分裂的国家，这个国家正在遭受大流行的痛苦，这场大流行已经导致美国超过23.8万人死亡。特朗普将疫情的影响降至最低，加剧了分裂，他甚至还没有说过他是否会承认选举结果。
拜登还将不得不圈养难以驾驭的民主党，该党进步派和中间派之间的紧张关系尚未解决。
然而，两党议员--包括加州众议院议长南希·佩洛西(Nancy Pelosi)等建制派民主党人，佛蒙特州参议员伯尼·桑德斯(Bernie Sanders)等进步人士，以及前佛罗里达州州长杰布·布什(Jeb Bush)和亚利桑那州参议员约翰·麦凯恩(John McCain)的遗孀辛迪·麦凯恩(Cindy McCain)等共和党人--很快对拜登和哈里斯预计的胜利表示祝贺。
前总统巴拉克·奥巴马在一份声明中表示，他不可能以此为荣来祝贺他的前副总统。
我知道他会把每一个美国人的最大利益放在心上，不管他是否得到了他们的选票。所以我鼓励每一个美国人给他一个机会，给予他你们的支持，奥巴马说。
犹他州共和党参议员米特·罗姆尼(Mitt Romney)在推特上祝贺拜登和哈里斯，并补充说，他和他的妻子都知道他们两人都是善意和令人敬佩的人。
拜登将于11月20日迎来78岁生日，他将成为美国历史上年龄最大的即将上任的总统。30多年前，拜登首次竞选美国最高职位。作为一名长期的温和派人士，他几十年来一直强调两党合作，他漫长的参议员生涯的典型特征是他愿意与共和党同僚一起工作。
拜登的过渡团队将于周一开始工作--继续规划员工任命、行政命令和政策提案，以便他能在1月20日开始工作。
一位竞选消息人士告诉NBC新闻，预计他还将在周一任命他的冠状病毒特别工作组。该小组将由前美国卫生局局长Vivek Murthy博士、美国食品和药物管理局(Food And Drug Administration)前局长大卫·凯斯勒(David Kessler)博士以及耶鲁大学(Yale University)医学副教授马尔塞拉·努涅斯-史密斯(Marcella Nunez-Smith)博士共同担任主席。
进入周六，在NBC新闻追踪的预计选举人团计票结果中，拜登以253票对214票领先特朗普。拜登在两个关键州的得票率更高：亚利桑那州和佐治亚州，这两个州的竞选势均力敌。据NBC新闻报道，北卡罗来纳州也仍然势均力敌。
除了预计的选举人团选票，拜登还赢得了普选，他创造了美国历史上赢得最多选票的候选人的记录。
特朗普一再错误地宣布胜利，包括在拜登预计获胜的多个州。总统还重复了毫无根据的阴谋论，并试图质疑制表过程的完整性。
在美国全国广播公司新闻频道(NBC News)宣布竞选结果后发布的一份长篇声明中，挑衅的特朗普表示：我们都知道为什么乔·拜登急于假扮成胜利者，以及为什么他的媒体盟友如此努力地帮助他：他们不希望真相被曝光。
简单的事实是，这次选举远未结束。乔·拜登(Joe Biden)还没有被认证为任何州的获胜者，更不用说那些竞争激烈、即将进行强制重新计票的州，或者那些我们的竞选活动面临合法合法挑战的州，这些挑战可能决定最终的胜利者，他说。
特朗普补充说，从周一开始，我们的竞选团队将开始在法庭上起诉我们的案件，以确保选举法得到充分维护，合法的胜利者就座。
周六早上，特朗普被拍到在华盛顿附近弗吉尼亚州斯特林的特朗普国家高尔夫俱乐部打高尔夫球。
拜登还被其他几家主要新闻机构预测成为当选总统，包括CNN、ABC新闻、福克斯新闻和美联社。
拜登表达了对所有选票清点完毕后获胜的明确信心，自从周二投票结束以来，拜登经常发表讲话，因为这个焦虑的国家正在等待各州完成计票。
我们还没有最后的宣言，胜利。但这些数字告诉我们一个清晰而令人信服的故事，拜登在周五晚上的一次简短演讲中说。我们将赢得这场比赛。
全国93%的预期选票已经清点完毕，拜登在普选中以50.5%对47.7%的支持率领先特朗普，这与特朗普形成鲜明对比，特朗普在2016年赢得选举人团时输掉了普选。到目前为止，他获得的7450万张选票是所有总统候选人在美国赢得的最多选票。
这些预测不太可能削弱特朗普竞选团队在法庭上争取总统连任的努力。他的竞选团队已经提起了多起诉讼，其中几起已经被驳回，预计还会有更多诉讼。据NBC新闻报道，他的竞选团队还表示，将要求在威斯康星州重新计票，拜登在威斯康星州显然是胜利者。
特朗普不断引发对计票过程的毫无根据的担忧，他在Twitter上发帖，要求官员停止计票，并在分析人士认为剩余选票有利于民主党的地方不计票。周三凌晨，在数百万张选票仍未清点的情况下，特朗普谎称自己赢了。周四，他举行了一场新闻发布会，发表了一系列虚假的选举舞弊指控。
拜登曾在1988年和2008年竞选总统，在今年激烈的民主党初选中获胜，其中一度有20多名候选人，在一场全球流行病爆发之际，拜登成为该党的明显提名人。他很快就被迫将竞选活动虚拟起来，直到夏末才恢复竞选旅行。当他这样做的时候，他的活动主要是在社交距离遥远的小团体记者面前举行，并为支持者进行现场直播。
当大选终于在劳动节后如火如荼地启动时，接二连三的新闻事件给竞选注入了历史性的混乱和紧迫感。
新冠肺炎的感染再次开始飙升。最高法院先驱大法官露丝·巴德·金斯伯格因癌症去世，引发共和党人争先恐后地在选举日前由特朗普选定的保守派继任者艾米·科尼·巴雷特(Amy Coney Barrett)填补这一席位。
几天后，特朗普对新冠肺炎的诊断抢了特朗普和拜登之间激烈而好斗的第一场总统辩论的风头。特朗普在医院呆了几天，在那里接受了实验性的治疗，然后回到白宫，开始竞选活动。
几项民调显示，持续混乱的程度似乎影响了即将进入大选的关键摇摆州对特朗普的支持。
但是，随着周二选举之夜的投票结果出来，人们很快就发现，许多民调都没有达到目标，就像2016年选举周期的情况一样。特朗普在几个州的民调中表现优异，而邮寄选票的大幅激增以及限制官员何时开始处理选票的规定推迟了计票工作。这些选票压倒性地支持拜登。
NBC新闻预计特朗普将在包括佛罗里达州、俄亥俄州、爱荷华州和德克萨斯州在内的几个关键州获胜。
与此同时，拜登在包括密歇根州和威斯康星州在内的上中西部地区掀翻了一片蓝色战场，他在这些地方投入了大量时间和精力来宣传他的重建更好政策，这些政策针对的是2016年逃离民主党转投特朗普的工薪阶层选民。
拜登在担任奥巴马副总统八年后的第三次、也是最后一次、也是胜利的总统竞选，已经酝酿了近半个世纪。
拜登于1972年开始了他长达数十年的政治生涯，当时29岁的他是一名律师，仅以3100票的优势击败了特拉华州的一名共和党参议员。
在他当选参议员几周后，他的妻子和他们年幼的女儿在圣诞节购物时死于一场车祸。拜登的两个儿子博(Beau)和亨特(Hunter)坐在后座上，受了伤，但幸免于难。五年后，他与现任妻子吉尔结婚。
在接下来的36年里，他六次赢得连任，并在1988年和2008年两次竞选总统失败。然后，一位来自伊利诺伊州的年轻的第一任期参议员巴拉克·奥巴马(Barack Obama)选择他作为他的竞选伙伴。
然而，拜登在华盛顿的漫长记录在民主党初选和大选期间都受到了密切关注。
他起草了《针对妇女的暴力法案》(Violence Against Women Act)，帮助通过了攻击性武器禁令，并在奥巴马之前站出来支持同性婚姻，但他也投票支持授权伊拉克战争，并是1994年一项有争议的犯罪法案的主要作者，该法案导致美国黑人和棕色人种遭到大规模监禁。
作为参议院司法委员会主席，他主持了最高法院大法官克拉伦斯·托马斯(Clarence Thomas)的确认听证会。拜登多年来一直因为处理安妮塔·希尔关于托马斯性骚扰她的证词而受到严厉批评，托马斯否认了这一点。
拜登曾认真考虑过在2016年挑战希拉里·克林顿的总统候选人提名，但在儿子博因癌症去世后，他最终决定放弃竞选。这一损失对拜登打击很大，他写了一本书，讲述自己的悲痛，《爸爸，答应我：充满希望、艰辛和目标的一年》。
自从特朗普在2016年意外获胜以来，拜登一直直言不讳地批评总统的政策和政治言论。他在2019年4月启动了2020年的竞选活动，将自己的愿景与特朗普对抗，将其视为一场争夺美国灵魂的战争。
拜登将自己参选的决定归因于特朗普对2017年弗吉尼亚州夏洛茨维尔(Charlottesville)暴力事件的回应。当时，在白人民族主义者和反抗议者的大规模集会上，一名白人至上主义者将自己的汽车撞向一个反对派团体，造成一人死亡。
更正(2020年11月7日晚上8：45。美国东部时间(ET)：本文的前一个版本错误地陈述了特朗普国家高尔夫俱乐部(Trump National Golf Club)的位置。它在弗吉尼亚州的斯特林，而不是华盛顿特区。
note_en:
WILMINGTON, Del. — Joe Biden became president-elect Saturday after winning the pivotal state of  Pennsylvania, NBC News projected.
 Biden had amassed 279 Electoral College votes after winning Pennsylvania's 20 electors and Nevada's 6 electors, according to NBC News, surpassing the 270 needed to win the White House and defeat President Donald Trump.
   NBC News called the race in  Pennsylvania for Biden at 11:24 a.m. ET, bringing Biden's Electoral College vote total to 273 and allowing the network to call the election for him. Shortly after 2 p.m. ET, NBC News called the race in  Nevada for Biden.
 Biden's victory capped one of the longest and most tumultuous campaigns in modern history, in which he maintained an aggressive focus on Trump's widely criticized handling of the Covid-19 pandemic. A majority of voters said rising coronavirus case numbers were a significant factor in their votes, according to early results from the  NBC News Exit Poll of early and Election Day voters.
 Biden regularly criticized Trump as unfit for office and positioned his campaign as a "battle for the soul of America." He promised from the outset of his run to heal and unite the country if he won, and he made central to his closing message a pledge to represent both those who voted for him as well as those who didn't when he got to the White House.
 In a statement issued shortly after NBC News called the race, Biden said he was "honored" by the news and reiterated the calls for unity that had been a hallmark of his campaign speeches in recent weeks.
  "I am honored and humbled by the trust the American people have placed in me and in Vice President-elect Harris," Biden said.
 "With the campaign over, it's time to put the anger and the harsh rhetoric behind us and come together as a nation. It's time for America to unite. And to heal," he said. "We are the United States of America. And there's nothing we can't do, if we do it together."
 Biden will address the nation Saturday night at 8 p.m. ET from Wilmington, his campaign said.
 His running mate,  Sen. Kamala Harris of California, said in a tweet that "this election is about so much more" than Biden and herself. "It's about the soul of America and our willingness to fight for it. We have a lot of work ahead of us. Let's get started," she said. A subsequent tweet showed a video of her calling Biden to emotionally tell him: "We did it, Joe. You're going to be the next president of the United States."
  Celebrations — and protests — erupted across the country in the moments after the race was called. Crowds of Biden supporters quickly gathered in Times Square and Grand Army Plaza in New York, in Black Lives Matter Plaza near the White House in Washington, D.C., and outside the Pennsylvania Convention Center in Philadelphia.
  Pro-Trump gatherings protesting Biden's victory broke out in numerous locations, too, including outside the Michigan State Capitol, in Lansing, and outside the North Carolina State Capitol, in Raleigh.
 Biden will be sworn in as the 46th president on Jan. 20.  Harris will become the first female,  first Black and  first South Asian American vice president.
 As president, Biden will immediately be confronted with a bitterly divided nation in the throes of a pandemic that has already killed more than  238,000 people in the United States. Trump has aggravated the split by minimizing the effects of the pandemic, and he hasn't even said whether he would recognize the outcome of the election.
 Biden will also have to corral a fractious Democratic Party with unresolved tensions between its progressive and centrist wings.
  Nevertheless, lawmakers from both parties — including establishment  Democrats like House Speaker Nancy Pelosi of California and progressives like Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., as well as Republicans like former Florida Gov. Jeb Bush and Cindy McCain, the widow of Sen. John McCain of Arizona — quickly offered their congratulations to Biden and Harris for their projected victory.
 Former President Barack Obama said in a statement that he couldn't be "prouder" to congratulate his former vice president.
 "I know he'll do the job with the best interests of every American at heart, whether or not he had their vote. So I encourage every American to give him a chance and lend him your support," Obama said.
   Sen. Mitt Romney, R-Utah,  tweeted his congratulations to Biden and Harris, adding that he and his wife "know both of them as people of good will and admirable character."
 Biden, who turns 78 on Nov. 20 and will be the oldest incoming president in U.S. history, first ran for the country's highest office more than 30 years ago. A longtime moderate, he has stressed bipartisanship for decades, and his long Senate career was typified by his willingness to work across the aisle with Republican colleagues.
 Biden's transition team will be working Monday — continuing the task of planning staff appointments, executive orders and policy proposals to allow him to hit the ground running on Jan. 20.
 He is also expected to name his coronavirus task force Monday, a campaign source told NBC News. The team will be co-chaired by Dr. Vivek Murthy, a former U.S. surgeon general; Dr. David Kessler, former commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration; and Dr. Marcella Nunez-Smith, an associate professor of medicine at Yale University.
  Heading into Saturday,  Biden led Trump 253 to 214 in the projected Electoral College vote tally tracked by NBC News. Biden had higher  vote totals in two key states:  Arizona and  Georgia, where the races are too close to call.  North Carolina also remains too close to call, according to NBC News.
 In addition to the projected Electoral College vote, Biden also won the popular vote, and he set a record for winning the most votes of any candidate in U.S. history.
 Trump has repeatedly and falsely declared victory, including in multiple states where Biden is the projected winner. The president has also repeated unfounded conspiracy theories and tried to cast doubt on the integrity of the tabulation process.
 In a lengthy statement released after NBC News called the race,  a defiant Trump said, "We all know why Joe Biden is rushing to falsely pose as the winner, and why his media allies are trying so hard to help him: they don't want the truth to be exposed."
  "The simple fact is this election is far from over. Joe Biden has not been certified as the winner of any states, let alone any of the highly contested states headed for mandatory recounts, or states where our campaign has valid and legitimate legal challenges that could determine the ultimate victor," he said.
 Trump added, "Beginning Monday, our campaign will start prosecuting our case in court to ensure election laws are fully upheld and the rightful winner is seated."
 Trump was photographed golfing Saturday morning at Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Virginia, near Washington.
  Biden was also projected to become president-elect by several other major news organizations, including CNN, ABC News, Fox News and The Associated Press.
  Biden had expressed clear confidence in a victory once all the votes were counted, speaking regularly since polls closed Tuesday as an anxious nation waited for states to complete their tallies.
 "We don't have a final declaration, a victory yet. But the numbers tell us a clear and convincing story," Biden said in a brief  speech Friday night. "We're going to win this race."
 With 93 percent of the expected vote across the country counted, Biden led Trump by 50.5 percent to 47.7 percent in the popular vote, a contrast to Trump, who lost the popular vote in 2016 while winning the Electoral College. The 74.5 million votes that have so far been counted for him is the largest number of votes won in the U.S. by any presidential candidate.
 The projections aren't likely to dampen efforts by the Trump campaign to fight for the president's re-election in court. His campaign has  filed multiple lawsuits, several of which have  already been thrown out, and more are expected. His campaign has also said it would ask for a recount in Wisconsin, where Biden is the apparent winner, according to NBC News.
  Trump has continually generated unfounded fears about the vote tabulation process, firing off tweets demanding that officials halt counting and leave ballots uncounted in places where analysts think the remaining votes favor Democrats. In the early hours of Wednesday, with millions of votes still uncounted,  Trump falsely claimed that he had won. On Thursday, he held a news conference where he  made a series of false election fraud claims.
 Biden, who ran for president in 1988 and 2008, emerged victorious from a bruising Democratic primary this year, which at one point included more than 20 candidates, becoming the party's apparent nominee just as a global pandemic was erupting. He was quickly forced to take his campaign virtual and didn't resume campaign travel until the end of the summer. When he did, his events were largely in front of small, socially distanced groups of reporters and livestreamed for supporters.
 When the general election finally kicked into high gear after Labor Day, an avalanche of news events injected historic chaos and urgency into the race.
 Covid-19 infections began to soar again. The pioneering  Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg died of cancer, touching off a rush by Republicans to fill the seat with Trump's chosen conservative successor, Amy Coney Barrett, before Election Day.
  A bitter and combative first presidential debate between Trump and Biden was upstaged by Trump's diagnosis of Covid-19 days later. Trump spent several days in the hospital, where he received experimental treatments, before returning to the White House and to the campaign trail.
 The level of sustained chaos appeared to take its toll on support for Trump in key swing states heading into the election, according to several polls.
 But as returns came in on election night Tuesday, it quickly became clear that many polls missed the mark, as was the case in the 2016 cycle.  Trump overperformed polls in several states, while a massive surge in mail-in ballots and rules restricting when officials could begin processing them delayed the vote counts. Those votes were overwhelmingly for Biden.
 NBC News projected Trump as the winner in several battleground states, including Florida, Ohio, Iowa and Texas.
  Biden, meanwhile, flipped blue Upper Midwest battlegrounds, including Michigan and Wisconsin, where he devoted particular time and energy promoting his "Build Back Better" policies targeting working-class voters who'd fled the Democratic Party for Trump in 2016.
 Biden's third, final and victorious presidential run after eight years as Obama's vice president was nearly a half-century in the making.
 Biden began his decadeslong political career in 1972 as a 29-year-old lawyer who beat a Republican U.S. senator from Delaware by only 3,100 votes.
 A few weeks after he was elected to the Senate, his wife and their infant daughter were killed in a car crash while Christmas shopping. Biden's two sons, Beau and Hunter, were in the backseat and were injured but survived. He married his current wife, Jill, five years later.
  Over the next 36 years, he won re-election six times — and ran unsuccessfully for president twice, in 1988 and 2008. Then, a young, first-term senator from Illinois named Barack Obama tapped him to be his running mate.
 Biden's lengthy record in Washington, however, came under intense scrutiny during both the Democratic primary and the general election.
 He authored the Violence Against Women Act, helped pass an assault weapons ban and came out in favor of same-sex marriage before Obama, but he also voted in favor of authorizing the Iraq War and was a lead author of a  controversial 1994 crime bill that contributed to the mass incarceration of Black and brown Americans.
 As chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, he presided over Justice Clarence Thomas' confirmation hearing to the Supreme Court. Biden over the years has been harshly criticized for his handling of Anita Hill's testimony that Thomas sexually harassed her, which Thomas denied.
  Biden seriously considered challenging Hillary Clinton for the presidential nomination in 2016, but he ultimately decided to forgo a run in the wake of his son Beau's death to cancer. The loss hit Biden hard, and he wrote a book about his grief, "Promise Me, Dad: A Year of Hope, Hardship, and Purpose."
 Since Trump's unexpected win in 2016, Biden has been a vocal critic of the president's policies and political rhetoric. He launched his 2020 campaign in April 2019, pitching his vision against Trump's as a "battle for the soul of America."
 Biden has attributed his decision to run to Trump's response to the 2017 violence in Charlottesville, Virginia, where, during a large gathering of white nationalists and counterprotesters, a white supremacist rammed his car into an opposition group, killing one person.
 CORRECTION (Nov. 7, 2020, 8:45 p.m. ET): A previous version of this article misstated the location of Trump National Golf Club. It is in Sterling, Virginia, not Washington, D.C.
title: 乔·拜登击败特朗普，宣布赢得美国总统选举
title_en: Defeating Trump, Joe Biden Declared Winner of US Presidential Elections

note:
从2019年8月开始，你可能已经在你的Facebook新闻订阅中看到一则广告，要求你参加一个新闻测验。例如，如果你不知道谁控制了参议院--大约30%的人不知道--你就会被归类为最有说服力的人，你就会成为这类实验中规模最大、最复杂的一部分。
在互联网上，我们一直在接受隐藏的A/B测试，但这一次也是政治武器的一部分：一个由Facebook老手、数据书呆子和计算社会科学家组成的团队打造的数百万美元的工具包，决心击败唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)。我们的目标是使用微定向广告、跟踪调查和无与伦比的数据集来赢得几个关键州的关键选民的支持：上次出人意料地成群结队地走出来或呆在家里的低学历选民，这些选民可能会决定另一场具有重大意义的选举。
到了今年春天，这个代号为“晴雨表”的项目似乎取得了成效。在两个月的时间里，数据科学家发现，向某些可能的特朗普选民展示某些Facebook广告会使他们对总统的支持率下降3.6%。在疯狂的最后几圈，他们着眼于激励另一个关键的摇摆州选民群体-倾向于民主党的年轻选民，主要是女性和有色人种-他们可能会推动乔·拜登(Joe Biden)走向胜利。
“我们已经能够真正理解如何与政治知识水平较低的人沟通，这些人往往会被政治过程忽视，”全数字进步非营利性机构的数据和广告专家詹姆斯·巴恩斯(James Barnes)说，他帮助建立了“晴雨表”。这是人们熟悉的领域：巴恩斯曾在Facebook的广告团队工作多年，2016年是帮助特朗普竞选团队在Facebook掀起一场风暴的“内嵌者”。去年，他离开了Facebook，决心用他久经考验的战术来扳倒他的前客户。
巴恩斯说：“我们已经找到了方法来找到正确的新闻摆在他们面前，我们也找到了方法来理解什么是有效的，什么是无效的。”“如果你把所有这些结合在一起，你就会得到一种真正有效的方法，这就是我们正在做的事情。”
随着疫情将选民粘在手机和屏幕上，并给竞选和投票增加了前所未有的障碍，2020年的数字军备竞赛变得比许多人预期的更大、更丑陋。根据响应性政治中心(Center For Responsive Politics)的数据，总统和国会竞选团队今年在数字广告上花费了超过10亿美元，这已经成为历史上代价最高的选举。在最后几周，在线广告购物狂潮变得如此激烈，以至于在许多关键州，YouTube的广告都销售一空。
总统有一个良好的开端。根据对Facebook广告档案的分析，除了他的前竞选经理所说的数据和竞选基础设施的“死星”之外，特朗普竞选团队在数字广告上投入的资源远远超过拜登的努力，到目前为止，在Facebook广告上的支出高达1.42亿美元，超过了拜登8900万美元的支出。
许多外部反特朗普团体，如首字母缩略词，已经花费了数百万美元来填补这一空白。今年早些时候，优先美国和变化之色发起了一项耗资2400万美元的数字广告活动，旨在刺激摇摆州的黑人选民。另外两个最大的进步政治行动委员会美国桥(American Bridge)和联合国家(Unite The Country)已经利用迈克·布隆伯格(Mike Bloomberg)的政治广告技术初创公司鹰鱼(Hawkfish)在选举日发起了自己的数据丰富的数字攻击。首字母缩略语最先问世，被认为是民主党最先进的数字广告项目。到大选时，它承诺将在Facebook、谷歌、Instagram、Snapchat、Hulu、Roku、维亚康姆(Viacom)、潘多拉(Pandora)以及其他任何有价值的选民身上花费7500万美元。
在一年的时间里，这些钱被用于瞄准密歇根州、宾夕法尼亚州、威斯康星州、亚利桑那州和北卡罗来纳州的低信息选民，但到了夏末，晴雨表团队发现它的说服力正在减弱；他们猜测，他们不能再让总统的支持率下降了。因此，这个首字母缩写将这笔钱重新分配给了另一个关键的低信息选民受众：新的或不太可能倾向于民主党的人，他们被认为对拜登和他的竞选伙伴参议员卡玛拉·哈里斯(Kamala Harris)不感兴趣。晴雨表的科学家已经在6个州确认了180万这样的选民，其中大部分是年龄在35岁以下的有色人种女性，这些女性来自首字母缩写最初的5个目标州，还有佐治亚州。
在最后冲刺阶段，Facebook每周的广告收入超过100万美元，“我们正在努力激发他们的热情，”该公司负责沟通的副总裁凯尔·萨普(Kyle Tharp)说。
尽管乐观的民调和创纪录的早期投票率，首字母缩写的战斗从来都不会是一件容易的事。这些选民被认为是最不兴奋的，虽然首字母缩略语将他们定义为最容易说服的人，但他们也很容易受到能让选民呆在家里的那种废话的影响。研究表明，低信息选民不仅不太可能投票，而且更有可能相信谎言；有时他们被称为“错误信息选民”。用谎言和恐惧吓阻选民，可能比用事实和希望激励他们更容易。例如，关于投票的虚假说法在Twitter上或通过匿名短信传播要比纠正要容易得多。
胡说八道没完没了。虽然特朗普团队在摇摆州铺天盖地地投放反拜登的广告，意在挫伤民主党人的热情(例如，攻击他和哈里斯在刑事司法改革方面的记录)，但这位候选人和其他许多人正在发动混合战争，推动有关贩卖儿童的阴谋的极端传言，以及关于选民欺诈和邮寄选票的谎言--这是削弱已经很难激励选民的信心和意志的便捷方式。
特朗普竞选团队的一名高级官员在2016年大选前几天对彭博社(Bloomberg)表示：“我们正在进行三次大规模的选民压制行动。”他指的是一系列正在消失的“黑暗帖子”，这些帖子把希拉里·克林顿描绘成种族主义者，针对白人自由主义者、年轻女性和非裔美国人。俄罗斯人采取了一种更直接的方式：在使用Facebook的工具识别出有色人种群体后，一个俄罗斯支持的页面在选举日用微目标瞄准了他们：“没有人代表黑人。”不要去投票。“。
这些都是巴恩斯和他的团队试图激励的选民类型中的一些。“我认为，从目标的角度来看，我们不仅试图填补其他(进步运动)无法填补的空白，”他说，“我们还试图找到那些我们认为很容易听到这些信息的人，并将事实摆在他们面前。”
巴恩斯有着一套特殊的技能和一种安静而强烈的紧迫感。他上一次用经过无情考验的Facebook广告和海量数据瞄准易受影响的选民是在特朗普竞选团队的大数据数字运营项目阿拉莫项目(Project Alamo)内。巴恩斯渴望将2016年抛在脑后，但他也欣然承认，他当时所做的工作是“开创性的”。特朗普有他的推特，但之前没有一个竞选团队像特朗普那样使用Facebook来说服选民，找到更多的选民，并筹集资金。有时，一美元的广告支出会转化为2美元或3美元的捐款。Facebook当时的广告技术主管在一份内部备忘录中写道，这是“我见过的最好的一次来自广告商的数字广告宣传活动。”。。。他们只是使用了我们必须使用的工具，向每个人展示了合适的创意。“。在一条后来被删除的推文中，特朗普的数字总监称巴恩斯为“我们的MVP”。
巴恩斯一生都是共和党人，但他不喜欢特朗普；他说他最终把票投给了克林顿。这次选举以及他在选举中扮演的角色让他感到不安，于是他离开了Facebook的政治广告团队，转而与该公司的商业客户合作。他了解到，剑桥分析公司(Cambridge Analytica)是如何付钱给几名研究人员--其中包括一名Facebook后来聘用的研究人员--让他们为数千名Facebook用户收集Facebook测验数据，并利用8700万Facebook个人资料来激励一些不太可能投票的人，压制另一些人。(巴恩斯曾与剑桥分析公司(Cambridge Analytica)的员工一起工作，但他表示，当时他不知道有任何压制选民的努力或非法获得的数据。)
在特朗普当选总统及其后果之后，巴恩斯帮助Facebook制定了一些选举诚信倡议(Facebook的举措之一是停止让像他这样的员工参与竞选活动)，甚至坐下来接受了美国证券交易委员会(Securities And Exchange Commission)和当时的特别顾问罗伯特·穆勒(Robert Mueller)的长时间采访。去年，在进行了一些自我反省(其中一些是在秘鲁)之后，巴恩斯登记为民主党人，离开了Facebook，开始研究一种对抗特朗普的方法。他联系了Facebook的另一位前政治广告专家塔滕达·穆萨帕蒂克(Tatenda Musapatike)，后者加入了Facebook的首字母缩写，担任竞选高级总监。他说：“我知道我有一套额外的经验和天赋，可以帮助我成为这个项目的一部分。”
首字母缩写和政治行动委员会Pacronym由民主党策略师Tara McGowan于2017年创立，目的是对抗特朗普的在线支出优势，以及《纽约客》所称的他在Facebook上的霸主地位。作为一家非营利性组织，虽然Pacronym披露了捐赠者的身份，但该组织并未披露捐赠者的身份。这两个组织7500万美元的战斗计划得到了加州巨擘名人录的支持：美元剃须俱乐部创始人迈克尔·杜宾、劳伦·鲍威尔·乔布斯、史蒂文·斯皮尔伯格，以及风险投资家里德·霍夫曼和迈克尔·莫里茨。据《华尔街日报》报道，Facebook前产品官克里斯·考克斯(Chris Cox)也是捐赠者和非正式顾问。
巴恩斯用他们的钱组建了一个八人的广告技术梦之队：硅谷的老兵以及计算社会科学和机器学习方面的专家。巴恩斯从Facebook招募的数据科学家所罗门·梅辛是社交媒体和政治方面的专家：在Facebook，他在2012年大选前进行的一项实验似乎表明，在用户的新闻馈送中推广更多新闻故事可以提高他们的政治意识，提高他们在投票中的投票率。Facebook的类似实验表明，朋友在选举日的提醒也可以推动人们去投票；今年，Facebook表示，它的News Feed提醒已经导致440万美国人登记投票。
在某些方面，巴恩斯和他的团队重建了Brand Lift，这是Facebook上的一项广告优化功能，巴恩斯帮助特朗普竞选团队以前所未有的规模利用了这一功能。(作为2016年后选举诚信改革的一部分，Facebook取消了针对政治广告商的这一功能。)。通过向看过其广告的用户发送跟踪调查(通常是在广告发布后一周)，晴雨表团队收集了关于哪些内容、叙述和信使在一系列问题上最有效地影响总统支持率、选民热情和投票选择的数据。例如，为了说服潜在的特朗普选民，晴雨表发现最具吸引力的不是党派新闻网站、高产量的广告，也不是广告，而是来自主流新闻来源的报道。巴恩斯说：“不是带有炒作的新闻内容，甚至不是社论新闻内容，而是关于总统的新闻内容。”
巴恩斯认为，即使是远程工作，晴雨表也比他为特朗普竞选团队帮助开发的说服努力领先了数光年。他说：“与现在相比，我们在2016年只是在黑暗中拍摄。”“我无法想象特朗普竞选团队现在会做类似的事情。”
对巴恩斯来说，首字母缩略词对Facebook的咄咄逼人的态度，以及晴雨表的存在，不仅是个人的，而且与他的前雇主有关：Facebook不仅没有有效地监管虚假信息和虚假信息，反而帮助加速了这一过程。
“我们所做的工作如此重要的原因之一就是提醒那些信息不多的选民--我们有很多证据表明他们的观点更容易改变，把主流的、经过事实核查的新闻放在人们面前，帮助他们了解世界上的实际情况--是因为Facebook做出的所有决定，尤其是在过去几年里做出的决定。”
我们知道特朗普会试图撒谎、作弊和窃取这场选举。这就是他一生都在做的事。但我们谁也想不到的是，Facebook会在多大程度上帮助他。Pic.twitter.com/u9TCsD0Efk
巴恩斯仍然对Facebook在2018年决定将布莱巴特放在一个专门报道“深度报道和消息来源”的版块感到困扰，这是为了代表马克·扎克伯格所说的“多元化观点”的努力的一部分。在维基百科上，由于不准确和煽动性的报道，布莱巴特被列入了不可靠的消息来源名单，不能被引用。
他说：“在很长一段时间里，这个平台的发展鼓励了来自右翼出版商的大量可疑真实信息的大量涌现--我显然在那里呆了一段时间--如果不是完全错误的话。右翼出版商往往不仅在订阅源上被优先考虑，而且在新闻选项卡上也得到了空间。”
虽然Facebook因拒绝在误导性的竞选广告上贴上事实核查标签而受到抨击，但来自可疑的右翼页面和网站(如布莱巴特(Breitbart)、每日电讯报(Daily Wire)和联邦党(The Federist))的有机内容一直在炮制自己的政治谎言。有时，Facebook会修改规则，以适应受欢迎的保守派媒体。
萨普说：“我认为这次选举最大的新闻之一是Facebook如何容忍--不仅是容忍，而且是优先考虑--右翼错误信息的传播是有机的，而不是通过广告。”“你知道，如果特朗普能做到这一点，这在很大程度上要归功于Facebook上流传的这类东西。”而Facebook只是耸耸肩，表现得就像‘战利品归胜利者所有’，‘他们知道如何使用我们的平台’之类的。
“所以，”巴恩斯补充道，“我们还有很多东西要与之抗争。”
但是，尽管巴恩斯使用了一些帮助特朗普的武器，但他煞费苦心地强调，与另一方不同，首字母缩写的大炮只是“事实”。他说：“我为我们运行的这个项目感到如此自豪，为我们的团队和所有工具感到自豪的原因之一是，我们正在做的事情没有任何误导。”我们甚至不需要这样做，因为我们只是把事实摆在人们面前。“。
首字母缩略词可能会说它不是肮脏的把戏，但它咄咄逼人的做法仍然令人惊讶。去年，它因在关键州推出名为Courier NewsRoom的新闻网站网络而招致抨击，该网络的推出部分是为了制衡右翼新闻机构的生态系统。新闻监督机构NewsGuard表示，这些网站“不够透明”，“精心挑选事实来推进民主党的叙事”。但是，虽然信使新闻室是党派性质的，但它并不像一些人所指责的那样，发布虚假的报道。
根据《晴雨表》的建议，尽管该公司在Facebook上推出了自己新闻网站的内容，但其主要关注的是来自更可信来源的提振新闻。晴雨表还发现，在可能较年长的特朗普选民中，保守派评论员的内容可能特别有说服力。例如，当该团队从《大西洋月刊》(The Atlantic)报道特朗普对退伍军人的贬低言论，向其潜在的特朗普选民发出信息时，他们发现这对特朗普的支持率没有重大影响。但福克斯新闻(Fox News)的一篇文章证实了特朗普的言论，这是一个更有说服力的信使。
正如一些媒体比其他媒体更受信任一样，首字母缩略词的做法也是基于这样一个前提，即朋友比陌生人更有说服力。这个想法是关系组织的核心，这是皮特·巴蒂吉格(Pete Buttigiig)竞选活动首创的一种策略，包括让支持者给他们的家人、朋友和邻居发信息，鼓励他们投票。特朗普和拜登的应用都要求用户上传他们的联系人列表，然后竞选团队就可以用这些列表来识别哪些人可能是目标，以及应用用户可以通过个性化信息瞄准哪些人。
为了通过喧嚣来吸引有说服力的选民，该组织及其合作伙伴黑人政治行动委员会(Black PAC)强化了一些信息，强调黑人领导人为选民带来的好处、对黑人生命也是命(Black Lives Matter)运动的广泛支持，以及拜登和哈里斯在社会和种族正义方面的历史。其他战略家和超级政治行动委员会也发起了旨在打击虚假信息和压制选民的努力。
巴恩斯说，很难说首字母缩略词关于选民说服的任何发现有多明确：没有其他人在进行或披露这样的实验。而且没有其他人拥有这样的数据。它是否全部奏效，可能只有在计票后才能知道，无论是什么时候。
选举结束后，缩写和晴雨表计划更多地与公众分享他们的经验教训，他们已经经常与进步的合作伙伴组织这样做。(法律禁止外部团体与竞选活动本身进行协调。)。萨普说：“我们的使命很大一部分是为进步运动建立力量，这意味着确保研究和从这一周期中学到的东西不会在竞选结束后就消失了。”
如果发生竞争激烈的选举或其他混乱，他们将如何处理自己的数字武器库？萨普说：“我们已经并将继续就这一问题与团体进行大量对话。”但他没有透露更多细节。
为了防止最后一刻的选举劫持，Facebook禁止在美国竞选活动的最后一周发布新的政治广告。上个月，该公司似乎屈服于多年的压力，宣布将在选举后无限期地完全停止销售政治广告。(Facebook管理人员曾表示，这些广告只占其营收的一小部分；根据其财务报告和广告档案，过去三个月，该公司报告称从政治广告中获利6.25亿美元，占其第二季度美国营收的7%。)。
冻结政治广告，特别是在竞选的最后一周，已经点燃了双方的挫败感。(周四，Facebook承认，一个技术错误导致两个政党的广告都被不当暂停。)。策略师麦高恩说，广告禁令阻碍了首字母缩略语和其他组织对抗政治错误信息的能力，他称最后一周的广告冻结是一种“肤浅和危险的公关举措”。
另一方面，Facebook禁令的部分原因是为了防止滥用政治微目标的蔓延。McGowan承认存在风险。她在最近一期的缩略语播客节目中说：“我们还必须注意这里的道德问题，那就是：这些工具可能被用于邪恶的目的。”他说：“它们既可以用来做好事，也可以用来做坏事。。。。当你有目标工具和测量工具时，你就可以确定如何在这种环境中利用错误信息来操纵人。“
这些工具因大量不受监管的个人数据而得到增强。两个政党--不仅仅是共和党和特朗普--都建立了庞大的数据武器库和信息交换；这是首字母缩略词这样的组织开始建立他们的目标Facebook用户名单的一种方式。除了正在收集的关于选民的海量信息外，首字母缩略词没有披露它购买或许可了哪些数据。但与其他资金雄厚的超级政治行动委员会和竞选活动一样，它可以访问史无前例的州选民档案和个人信息池：从你的购买模式到你在社交媒体上的帖子，再到你的教堂，层层排列着人工智能构建的预测你性格的分数。在错误的人手中，这些成堆的个人信息可能就是法学教授保罗·欧姆所说的da。
.
note_en:
Starting in August 2019, you may have seen an ad in your Facebook news feed asking you to take a news quiz. If you didn’t know who controlled the Senate, for instance—about 30% of people didn’t—you would be classified as most persuadable, and you would become part of one of the largest and most sophisticated experiments of its kind.
   On the internet, we’re subject to hidden A/B tests all the time, but this one was also part of a political weapon: a multimillion-dollar tool kit built by a team of Facebook vets, data nerds, and computational social scientists determined to defeat Donald Trump. The goal is to use microtargeted ads, follow-up surveys, and an unparalleled data set to win over key electorates in a few critical states: the low-education voters who unexpectedly came out in droves or stayed home last time, the voters who could decide another monumental election.
 By this spring, the project, code named Barometer, appeared to be paying off. During a two-month period, the data scientists found that showing certain Facebook ads to certain possible Trump voters lowered their approval of the president by 3.6%. For the frantic final laps, they’ve set their sights on motivating another key group of swing-state voters—young Democratic-leaning voters, mostly women and people of color—who could push Joe Biden to victory.
 “We’ve been able to really understand how to communicate with folks who have lower levels of political knowledge, who tend to be ignored by the political process,” says James Barnes, a data and ads expert at the all-digital progressive nonprofit Acronym, who helped build Barometer. This is familiar territory: Barnes spent years on Facebook’s ads team, and in 2016 was the “embed” who helped the Trump campaign take Facebook by storm. Last year, he left Facebook and resolved to use his battle-tested tactics to take down his former client.
 “We have found ways to find the right news to put in front of them, and we found ways to understand what works and doesn’t,” Barnes says. “And if you combine all those things together, you get a really effective approach, and that’s what we’re doing.”
 As the pandemic has glued voters to their phones and screens and added unprecedented hurdles to campaigning and voting, the 2020 digital arms race has become bigger and uglier than many had expected. The presidential and congressional campaigns have spent more than $1 billion on digital ads this year, according to the  Center for Responsive Politics, during what’s become the costliest election in history. In the final weeks, the online ad shopping spree has grown so intense that in many battleground states,  YouTube ads are sold out.
 The president had a running start. On top of what his former campaign manager  called a “Death Star” of data and campaign infrastructure, the Trump campaign has devoted far more of its resources to digital advertising than the Biden effort, and spent a whopping $142 million on Facebook ads so far, outpacing Biden’s outlay of $89 million, according to an analysis of the Facebook ad archive.
  A multitude of outside anti-Trump groups such as Acronym have spent millions more to fill in the gaps. Earlier this year, Priorities USA and Color of Change launched a $24 million digital advertising campaign aimed at exciting Black voters in swing states. American Bridge and Unite the Country, two of the other largest progressive PACs, have tapped Mike Bloomberg’s  political ad tech startup, Hawkfish to wage their own data-rich digital onslaughts through Election Day. Acronym was first out of the gate, and is thought to be the Democrats’ most advanced digital advertising project. By the election it promises to have spent $75 million on Facebook, Google, Instagram, Snapchat, Hulu, Roku, Viacom, Pandora, and anywhere else valuable voters might be found.
 For a year that money went toward targeting low-information voters in Michigan, Pennsylvania, Wisconsin, Arizona, and North Carolina, but by the end of the summer, the Barometer team saw its persuasion powers diminishing; they guessed that they couldn’t budge the president’s approval rating any lower. So Acronym redirected that cash to motivate another critical audience of low-information voters: new or unlikely Democratic-leaning people thought to be unexcited about Biden and his running mate, Senator Kamala Harris. Barometer’s scientists have identified 1.8 million such voters in six states—mostly women of color younger than 35 across Acronym’s original five target states, plus Georgia.
 With more than $1 million per week in Facebook ads during the homestretch, “we’re trying to boost their enthusiasm,” says Kyle Tharp, Acronym’s VP of communications.
  Despite upbeat polls and record  early turnout numbers, Acronym’s battle was never going to be easy. These voters are thought to be some of the least-excited, and while Acronym has identified them as the easiest to persuade, they are also highly susceptible to the sort of BS that can keep voters home. Research has shown that low-information voters are not only less likely to vote but more likely to believe falsehoods; sometimes they’re called “misinformation voters.” And deterring voters with falsehoods and fear may be easier than motivating them with facts and hope. A false claim about voting, for instance, is much easier to spread on Twitter—or by anonymous text message—than it is to correct.
 And there’s no end to the BS. While Team Trump floods swing states with anti-Biden ads meant to dampen enthusiasm among Democrats (attacks on his and Harris’s record on criminal justice reform, for instance), the candidate and many others are waging hybrid war, pushing extreme rumors of child-trafficking cabals and  falsehoods about voter fraud and mail-in ballots—a handy way to erode the confidence and will of already hard-to-motivate voters.
 “We have three major voter suppression operations underway,” a senior Trump campaign official  told  Bloomberg in the days before the 2016 election, referring to a barrage of disappearing “dark posts” that painted Hillary Clinton as a racist and targeted white liberals, young women, and African Americans. The Russians took a more direct approach: After identifying groups of people of color using Facebook’s tools, one Russian-backed page microtargeted them with an ad on Election Day: “No one represents Black people. Don’t go to vote.”
  These are some of the same types of voters that Barnes and his team are trying to motivate. “I think we’re trying to not just fill the gap that other [progressive campaigns] aren’t filling, from a targeting perspective,” he says, “but we’re also trying to find the people who we think are pretty vulnerable to hearing this information and get the facts in front of them.”
  Barnes comes with a special skill set and a quiet, fierce sense of urgency. The last time he targeted impressionable voters with relentlessly tested Facebook ads and reams of data was inside Project Alamo, the Trump campaign’s  big-data digital operation. Barnes is eager to put 2016 behind him, but readily acknowledges the work he did then was “groundbreaking.” Trump had his Twitter, but no campaign had before used Facebook to persuade voters, find more of them, and raise money like Trump’s had. At times a dollar in ad spend translated to donations of $2 or $3. Facebook’s then-head of ad technology wrote in an  internal memo that it was “the single best digital ad campaign I’ve ever seen from any advertiser. . . . They just used the tools we had to show the right creative to each person.” In a since-deleted tweet, Trump’s digital director, called Barnes “our MVP.”
 Barnes had been a Republican all his life, but he did not like Trump; he says he ended up voting for Clinton. The election, and his role in it, left him unsettled, and he left Facebook’s political ads team to work with the company’s commercial clients. He learned how  Cambridge Analytica had paid a couple of researchers—including  one whom Facebook would later hire— to serve data-scraping Facebook quizzes to thousands of Facebook users, and used a cache of 87 million Facebook profiles to motivate some unlikely voters and  suppress others. (Barnes worked alongside Cambridge Analytica employees but has said he wasn’t aware of any voter suppression efforts or ill-gotten data at the time.)
   In the wake of Trump’s election and its aftermath, Barnes helped Facebook develop some of its election integrity initiatives (one of Facebook’s moves was to stop embedding employees like him inside campaigns) and even sat down for lengthy interviews with the Securities and Exchange Commission and with then-Special Counsel Robert Mueller. Last year, after some soul-searching, some of it in Peru, Barnes registered as a Democrat, left Facebook, and began working on a way to fight Trump. He reached out to Tatenda Musapatike, another former political ads specialist at Facebook, who had joined Acronym as its senior director of campaigns. “I knew that I had an extra set of experiences and talents that could be helpful to help be a part of this,” he says.
  Acronym and a political action committee, Pacronym, were founded in 2017 by Democratic strategist Tara McGowan, in an effort to counter Trump’s online spending advantage and what  The New Yorker  called his Facebook juggernaut. As a nonprofit, Acronym does not disclose its donors, though Pacronym does, and the groups’ $75 million battle plan drew backing from a who’s who of California megadonors:  Dollar Shave Club founder Michael Dubin, Laurene Powell Jobs, Steven Spielberg, and venture capitalists Reid Hoffman and Michael Moritz. Chris Cox, Facebook’s former product officer, is also a donor and informal adviser, according to the   Wall Street Journal.
 Barnes has used their cash to build an eight-person ad tech dream team: vets of Silicon Valley along with experts in computational social science and machine learning. One of Barnes’s recruits from Facebook, data scientist Solomon Messing, is an expert in social media and politics: At Facebook, one experiment he ran ahead of the 2012 election  appeared to show that promoting more news stories in users’ news feeds increased their political awareness and their turnout at the polls. Similar Facebook experiments have shown that Election Day reminders from friends can also  push people to the polls; this year, Facebook  says its News Feed reminders have led 4.4 million Americans to register to vote.
 In some ways, Barnes and his team have rebuilt Brand Lift, an ad-optimization feature on Facebook that Barnes helped the Trump campaign leverage at unprecedented scale. (As part of its post-2016 election integrity reforms, Facebook removed the feature for political advertisers.) By sending follow-up surveys to users who have seen its ads, typically a week after the fact, the Barometer team has collected data on which content, narratives, and messengers are most effective at impacting presidential approval, voter enthusiasm, and vote choice, across a range of issues. To persuade potential Trump voters, for instance, Barometer has found the most traction not with partisan news sites, highly produced ads, or ads at all, but with stories from mainstream news sources. “Not news content with spin, not even editorialized news content, just news content about the president,” Barnes says.
   Even working remotely, Barnes believes Barometer is light years ahead of the persuasion efforts he helped develop for the Trump campaign. “We were just shooting in the dark in 2016 compared to what we’re doing now,” he says. “I cannot imagine that the Trump campaign is doing anything similar right now.”
  For Barnes, Acronym’s aggressive approach to Facebook, and Barometer’s very existence, isn’t just personal, but relates to his former employer: Facebook hasn’t only failed to effectively police misinformation and disinformation, but helped accelerate it.
  “One of the reasons that the work that we’re doing is so important, just reminding low-information voters—folks whom we have a lot of evidence that their opinions are easier to shift, and putting mainstream, fact-checked news in front of folks that help them understand what’s actually going on in the world—is because all of the decisions that Facebook has made, especially in the past few years.”
 We knew Trump would try to lie, cheat, and steal this election. That’s what he’s done his whole life. But what none of us could have expected is just how far Facebook would go to help him.  pic.twitter.com/u9TCsD0Efk
  Barnes is still bothered by Facebook’s 2018 decision to place Breitbart in a section devoted to “deeply reported and well-sourced” journalism, as part of an effort to represent  what Mark Zuckerberg called “a diversity of views.” On Wikipedia, Breitbart has been placed on  a list of unreliable sources not to be cited, due to instances of inaccurate and incendiary reporting.
 “The evolution of the platform has encouraged, over a very long time—I was there obviously for a while—just the vast proliferation of questionably true, if not just outright false information from right-wing publishers, who are often not just prioritized on the feed, but are given space on the news tab,” he says.
 While Facebook has come under fire for declining to place fact-checking labels on misleading campaign ads, organic content from dubious  right-wing pages and sites like Breitbart, The Daily Wire, and The Federalist have been pumping out their own political falsehoods. At times, Facebook has  bent its rules to accommodate popular conservative outlets.
 “I think one of the biggest stories of the election is how Facebook has tolerated—and not only just tolerated, but prioritized—the distribution of right-wing misinformation—organically, and not through advertising,” says Tharp. “You know, if Trump is able to pull this out, it will be in no small part due to that sort of stuff spreading on Facebook. And Facebook just shrugs and acts like, you know, ‘to the victors go the spoils,’ ‘they know how to use our platform,’ whatever.”
  “So,” adds Barnes, “we’ve got a lot to fight against.”
 But while Barnes is using some of the weapons that helped Trump, he’s at pains to emphasize that, unlike the other side, Acronym’s artillery is simply “the facts.” “One of the reasons I’m so proud of the program that we’ve run and so proud of the team and all the tools is there’s nothing misinforming about what we’re doing. We wouldn’t even need to, because we’re just putting the facts in front of people.”
  Acronym may say it’s above dirty tricks, but its aggressive approach has still raised eyebrows. Last year it  drew fire for launching a  network of news sites in battleground states called Courier Newsroom, which it launched in part to counterbalance an ecosystem of right-wing journalism outfits. NewsGuard, a journalism watchdog, said the websites are “insufficiently transparent” and “cherry-pick facts to advance a Democratic narrative.” But while Courier Newsroom is partisan, it does not, as  some have charged, publish false stories.
   Even as it pushes out its own news sites’ content on Facebook, Acronym has focused heavily on boosted news from more trusted sources, per Barometer’s recommendations. Among older possible Trump voters, Barometer has also found that content from conservative commentators can be particularly persuasive. For instance, when the team messaged its persuasion audience of potential Trump voters with reporting from  The Atlantic about the president’s disparaging comments concerning war veterans, they found no meaningful impact on his approval. But a Fox News article confirming Trump’s comments was a more persuasive messenger.
  Just as some media are more trusted than others, Acronym’s approach is also informed by the premise that friends are more persuasive messengers than strangers. The idea is core to  relational organizing, a tactic pioneered by the Pete Buttigieg campaign that involves getting supporters to message their family, friends, and neighbors to encourage them to vote. Both the Trump and Biden apps ask users to upload their contact lists, which campaigns can then use to identify people who are likely targets, and whom the app users can target with personalized messages.
  To reach its persuadable audience of voters through the noise, Acronym and Black PAC, one of its partners, have honed messages that emphasize the gains that Black leaders have made for voters, broad support for the Black Lives Matter movement, and Biden’s and Harris’s history of social and racial justice. Other strategists and super PACs have launched efforts aimed at combating misinformation and  voter suppression efforts.
  It’s hard to say how definitive any of Acronym’s findings about voter persuasion are: No one else is running or disclosing experiments like this, Barnes says. And no one else has this kind of data. Whether it all works may be known only after the votes are counted, whenever that is.
 After the election, Acronym and Barometer plan to share more of their lessons with the public at large, something they already do frequently with progressive partner organizations. (Law prohibits outside groups from coordinating with the campaigns themselves.) “A big part of our mission is to build power for the progressive movement, and that means making sure research and learnings from this cycle don’t just disappear once the campaign is over,” says Tharp.
 What will they do with their digital arsenal in the event of a contested election or other chaos? “We’ve had and continue to have lots of conversations with groups on this,” Tharp says, without going into details.
  To prevent last-minute election hijinks, Facebook has banned new political ads for the final week of the U.S. campaigns. And last month, the company appeared to cave to years of pressure when it announced that it would cease selling political ads altogether after the election, for an indefinite period. (Facebook officials have said these ads represent a small portion of its revenue; in the past three months, it reported earning $625 million from political ads, or 7% of its second-quarter U.S. revenue,  according to its financial reports and ad archive.)
 Freezing political ads, especially in the campaigns’ final week, has ignited frustration on both sides. (On Thursday, Facebook  acknowledged that a technical error had caused ads from both political parties to be improperly  paused.) An ad ban hobbles the ability of Acronym and other groups to counter political misinformation, says strategist  McGowan, who called the final-week ad freeze a “shallow and dangerous public relations move.”
  On the other hand, part of the reason for Facebook’s ban is to prevent the spread of abusive political microtargeting. McGowan acknowledges the risk. “We have to also note the moral question here, which is: These tools can be used for nefarious purposes,” she said on a recent episode of Acronym’s podcast. “They can be used for bad as well as they can be used for good. . . . And when you have targeting tools and you have measurement tools you can identify how to manipulate people with misinformation in this environment.”
 Those tools are enhanced by an ocean of  unregulated personal data. Both political parties—not just the GOP and Trump—have built large data arsenals and information exchanges; that’s one way groups like Acronym start building the lists of Facebook users they target. Apart from the mountains of information it is gathering on voters, Acronym has not disclosed what data it has purchased or licensed. But like other well-funded super PACs and campaigns, it can access an unprecedented pool of  state voter files and personal information: everything from your purchasing patterns to your social media posts to your church, layered with AI-built scores that predict your traits. In the wrong hands, these piles of personal information can be what law professor Paul Ohm  calls da
......
title: 前Facebook数据专家如何在定向反特朗普广告上花费7500万美元

旧金山--优步(Uber)帮助加州发动了一场耗资2亿美元的战争，目的是让司机继续担任承包商。既然投票措施已经通过，该公司表示，它的工作还没有完成。
这家叫车巨头的首席执行官周四表示，优步正寻求将这一模式推广到其他州，本周早些时候，竞争对手Lyft的一名高管也发表了类似的言论。22号提案将工人确立为独立合同工，取代了加州的一项法律，该法律旨在让包括叫车和送餐司机在内的零工成为享有全额福利的员工。
加州选民站在优步一边，将司机归类为承包商，拒绝了他们的福利。“展望未来，你会看到我们更加大声地支持…。。Uber首席执行官Dara Khosrowshahi说：“我们是第一个提出这种(独立承包商)加模式的人，司机理应享有灵活性和福利。”我们希望(与)其他州的政府进行对话。“。
22号提案获得了58%的选民的支持，它让司机成为独立的合同工，承诺提供最低工资的120%和医疗保健缴费，相当于《平价医疗法案》(Affordable Care Act)下雇主提供的平均水平的一半或100%。然而，与充分就业不同的是，福利是根据司机的活动时间计算的，扣除了他们登录应用程序时每周等待车费的潜在小时数。
优步和零工公司花费近2亿美元推翻了他们不喜欢的劳动法-这可能会奏效，工党倡导者强烈反对22号提案，称这是明显试图从工人手中夺取新的就业权利。
优步(Uber)、DoorDash和Insta等公司表示，他们需要保持合同模式，以保持在应用程序内允许短等待时间和低票价的灵活性。这些公司捐赠了2亿美元的竞选资金，以支持这一计划。
但高昂的成本也伴随着就业。优步(Uber)和Lyft的股价本周出现反弹，百分比涨幅达到两位数，原因是投资者对他们不必让司机成为员工的消息做出了反应。
优步首席财务官Nelson Chai表示：“这可能会导致5%的增长，以弥补增加的[成本]，”包括收益在内。“我们相信这将是可控的。”
在截至9月份的季度里，优步报告了11亿美元的季度亏损，比去年同期减少了6%。自2019年5月上市以来，该公司一直没有盈利。
加州2020年大选结果周三，Lyft首席政策官、曾在奥巴马政府担任交通部长的安东尼·福克斯(Anthony Foxx)在接受采访时表示，Lyft还将寻求在全国推广Prop 22车型。
他说：“我认为22号提案现在已经为我们创造了一个架构，让我们有可能与其他州和华盛顿的领导人进行讨论。”“我们认为22号提案现在创造了一种可复制、可规模化的模式.”

note_en:
SAN FRANCISCO — Uber helped wage a $200 million war in California to keep drivers as contractors. Now that the ballot measure has passed, the company says its work isn’t done.
       The ride-hailing giant’s CEO said Thursday that Uber is looking to expand the model to other states, joining an executive from rival Lyft who said something  similar earlier this week. Proposition 22 established workers as independent contractors, superseding a California law that aimed to make gig workers, including ride-hailing and food delivery drivers, employees with full benefits.
   California voters sided with Uber, denying drivers benefits by classifying them as contractors “Going forward, you’ll see us more loudly advocating for …. laws like Prop 22,” Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said, adding later: “We were the first to come forward with this [independent contractor]-plus model, the idea that drivers deserve flexibility plus benefits. We want to have a dialogue with governments [in] other states.”
 Prop 22, which was approved by  58 percent of voters, makes drivers independent contractors with promises of 120 percent of the minimum wage and a health-care contribution, equivalent to either half or 100 percent of the employer-provided average under the Affordable Care Act. Unlike full employment, however, benefits are calculated based on a driver’s active time, negating the potential hours per week they spend waiting for a fare while logged onto the apps.
Uber and gig companies spend nearly $200 million to knock down an employment law they don’t like — and it might work Labor advocates fiercely opposed Prop 22, saying it was a transparent attempt to snatch newly enshrined employment rights from workers.
 Companies such as Uber, DoorDash and Instacart, which contributed to the $200 million campaign war chest in support of it, said they needed to maintain their contract models to keep the flexibility that allow short wait times and low fares within their apps.
 But steep costs also come with employment. Uber and Lyft’s stocks have  rallied this week, logging percentage gains in the double digits as investors reacted to the news that they would not have to make drivers employees.
  “It will result in probably a five percent increase to cover the incremental [costs],” including benefits, said Uber chief financial officer Nelson Chai. “We do believe that it’ll be manageable.”
 Uber reported a $1.1 billion quarterly loss in the period that ended in September, 6 percent less than the figure reported in the same quarter a year ago. The company has not been profitable  since debuting on the stock market in May 2019.
California's 2020 election results Anthony Foxx, Lyft’s chief policy officer and the former secretary of transportation under President Barack Obama, said in an interview Wednesday that the company would also look to expand the Prop 22 model across the country.
 “I think Prop 22 has now created a structure for us to discuss with leaders in other states and Washington, potentially,” he said. “We think that Prop 22 has now created a model that can be replicated and can be scaled.”

民主党人马克·凯利(Mark Kelly)很快将成为美国国家航空航天局(NASA)第四位在国会任职的宇航员，此前他推翻了共和党人玛莎·麦克萨利(Martha McSally)在亚利桑那州的参议院席位。
根据亚利桑那州国务卿的网站，54岁的凯利赢得了超过52%的选票。在周三发布的一条推文中，他说，他对当选并担任已故参议员约翰·麦凯恩(John McCain)曾经担任的职位深感荣幸。
我深感荣幸的是，亚利桑那州人相信我会成为他们的下一任美国参议员，并担任参议员约翰·麦凯恩(John McCain)曾经担任过的这个席位。Pic.twitter.com/scNwzYpmoJ。
凯利是一名退役的美国海军上尉和宇航员，他已经执行了四次太空任务，其中包括2011年奋进号航天飞机的最后一次任务。他与前亚利桑那州国会议员加比·吉福兹(Gabby Giffords)结婚，2011年被枪杀，差点被杀。
他的同卵双胞胎斯科特·凯利也是一名退休宇航员。两人参与了美国国家航空航天局具有里程碑意义的双胞胎研究，斯科特在空间站呆了一年，科学家们将马克的生理数据收集回国进行比较。
周二晚上在图森露面时，凯利提到了他在太空的时光，称他的竞选活动是一项使命，因为计票工作仍在继续。
凯利说：619天前，当我们在此地发起这场运动时，我从未预料到会有多少亚利桑那州人参与到这项使命中来。这项任务并没有在最后一次投票计票时结束。这只是个开始。
就在凌晨1点之前，美联社(Associated Press)正式宣布了凯利的竞选。星期三早上。
凯利于2019年2月发起了他的竞选活动，他表示，他的竞选平台将强调负担得起的医疗保健、提高工资、增加就业、提振经济和应对气候变化。他也是一名枪支安全倡导者，自从他的妻子被枪击后，他一直特别直言不讳地表达了这一立场。
他的对手麦克萨利在2018年亚利桑那州参议员竞选中落败，但在该州州长任命她填补乔恩·凯尔(Jon Kyl)空出的席位后，她还是找到了一个席位。乔恩·凯尔在2018年8月麦凯恩去世后暂时填补了这个空缺。
凯利的胜利发生在民主党人乔·拜登(Joe Biden)被美联社(Associated Press)预测将赢得亚利桑那州的选举期间，这个在2016年大选中投票给唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)的州发生了翻天覆地的变化。1996年赢得亚利桑那州的比尔·克林顿(Bill Clinton)是最后一位赢得该州的民主党人。
分析人士猜测，这一转变至少与特朗普嘲弄前参议员麦凯恩有关。
当凯利宣誓就职时，他将是唯一一位飞上太空的现役国会议员。在他之前，有三名前NASA宇航员：前参议员约翰·格伦(John Glenn)、杰克·施密特(Jack Schmitt)和众议员杰克·斯威格特(Jack Swigert)。另外两名前国会议员-参议员杰克·加恩(Jack Garn)和众议员比尔·纳尔逊(Bill Nelson)-已经作为有效载荷专家飞入太空。
阿波罗13号宇航员约翰·斯威格特于1982年再次当选为众议院议员，但在上任前死于癌症。

note_en:
Democrat  Mark Kelly will soon be the fourth NASA astronaut to serve in Congress, after  flipping an Arizona Senate seat held by Republican Martha McSally.
 According to the Arizona Secretary of State's  website, Kelly, 54, won with more than 52 percent of the vote. In a tweet posted Wednesday, he said he was "deeply honored" to have been elected and to serve in the seat once held by the late Sen.  John McCain.
 I am deeply honored that Arizonans have trusted me to be their next United States Senator and to serve in this seat once held by Senator John McCain.  pic.twitter.com/scNwzYpmoJ
 A retired U.S. Navy captain and astronaut, Kelly has flown in four space missions, including the final mission of  Space Shuttle  Endeavour in 2011. He is married to former Arizona Congresswoman Gabby Giffords, was  shot and nearly killed in 2011.
  His identical twin Scott Kelly is also a retired astronaut. The two participated in NASA's landmark " twins study," in which Scott spent a year aboard the space station while scientists collected Mark's physiological data back home for comparison.
 In an  appearance Tuesday evening in Tucson, Kelly made allusions to his time in space, referring to his campaign as a "mission" as votes continued to be tallied.
 "When we launched this campaign from this very spot, right here, 619 days ago, I could have never anticipated just how many Arizonians would be part of this mission," said Kelly. "This mission does not end when the last vote is counted. It is only the beginning."
 The Associated Press officially called the race for Kelly just before 1 a.m. on Wednesday morning.
  Kelly  launched his campaign in Feb. 2019, running on a platform that he said would emphasize affordable healthcare, improving wages, increasing employment, bolstering the economy and addressing climate change. He is also a gun safety advocate, and has been particularly vocal about that stance since his wife was shot.
  His opponent, McSally, lost a race for the Arizona Senate in 2018, but found herself with a seat anyway after the state's governor appointed her to the seat vacated by Jon Kyl, who temporarily filled the seat after the  Aug. 2018 death of McCain.
  Kelly's win came during an election in which Democrat  Joe Biden is projected by the Associated Press to win Arizona, flipping a state that voted for  Donald Trump in the 2016 election. Bill Clinton, who won Arizona in 1996, had been the last Democrat to win the state.
 Analysts speculated that the flip had at least something to do with Trump's  mocking of former Sen. McCain.
 When he is sworn in, Kelly will be the only active member of Congress to have flown in space. He is preceded by three former NASA astronauts: former Sens. John Glenn and Jack Schmitt and Rep. Jack Swigert. Two other former members of Congress — Sen. Jake Garn and Rep. Bill Nelson — have flown in space as payload specialists.
 Apollo 13 astronaut John "Jack" Swigert was elected to the House of Representatives in 1982, but died of cancer before he could take office.

安全公司卡巴斯基(Kaspersky)今天表示，他们发现了RansomEXX勒索软件的Linux版本，这标志着Windows勒索软件的一个主要漏洞首次被移植到Linux上，以帮助进行有针对性的入侵。
RansomEXX是一种相对较新的勒索软件毒株，今年早些时候在6月份首次发现。
该勒索软件已被用来攻击德克萨斯州交通部、柯尼卡美能达、美国政府承包商泰勒技术公司、蒙特利尔的公共交通系统，最近还被用来攻击巴西的法院系统(STJ)。
RansomEXX是安全研究人员所称的大型猎人或人操作的勒索软件。这两个术语用来形容勒索软件集团，它们在知道一些公司或政府机构无法在恢复系统的同时，追捕大型目标，以求大赚一笔。
这些组织自己购买访问权或侵入网络，尽可能扩大对更多系统的访问权，然后手动部署他们的勒索软件二进制文件作为最终有效负载，以尽可能多地削弱目标的基础设施。
但在过去的一年里，这些组织的运作方式发生了范式转变。
许多勒索软件团伙已经意识到，首先攻击工作站并不是一笔有利可图的交易，因为公司往往会重新镜像受影响的系统，然后在不支付赎金的情况下继续前进。
近几个月来，在许多事件中，一些勒索软件团伙没有费心加密工作站，而是首先瞄准了公司网络中的关键服务器，因为他们知道，如果首先关闭这些系统，公司将无法访问其集中的数据宝藏，即使工作站没有受到影响。
RansomEXX帮派创建了他们的Windows勒索软件的Linux版本，这与今天许多公司的运营情况是一致的，许多公司在Linux上运行内部系统，而不是总是在Windows Server上运行。
从攻击者的角度来看，Linux版本是完全有意义的；他们总是寻求扩大和触及尽可能多的核心基础设施，以求削弱公司并要求更高的赎金。
我们从RansomEXX看到的可能很快就会成为一个行业定义的趋势，其他大型勒索软件集团也会在未来推出他们的Linux版本。
而且，这一趋势似乎已经开始。根据网络安全公司Emsisoft的说法，除了RansomEXX，Mespinoza(PYSA)勒索软件团伙最近还在他们最初的Windows版本的基础上开发了一个Linux变体。
但Linux勒索软件也不是独一无二的。在过去的几年里，其他勒索软件团伙也创造了Linux勒索软件菌株，比如Snatch Group。然而，这些组织都是小规模的行动，依靠垃圾邮件活动来感染受害者，很少成功，也没有像我们今天看到的当代勒索软件组织那样进行有针对性的入侵。
Emsisoft表示，他们检测到的RansomEXX Linux变种早在7月份就可以看到。由于大型猎人勒索软件团队的操作方式，配置系统来检测RansomEXX Linux变体并不是一个可靠的策略。当攻击者部署勒索软件时，他们已经拥有了一家公司的大部分网络。针对这些类型的入侵，公司可以采取的最佳策略是对网关设备应用安全补丁，并确保它们没有被错误配置为弱凭据或默认凭据，从而保护网络边界。
note_en:
Security firm Kaspersky said today that it discovered a Linux version of the RansomEXX ransomware, marking the first time a major Windows ransomware strain has been ported to Linux to aid in targeted intrusions.
     RansomEXX is a relatively new ransomware strain that was first spotted earlier this year in June.
 The ransomware has been used in attacks against the  Texas Department of Transportation,  Konica Minolta, US government contractor  Tyler Technologies,  Montreal's public transportation system, and, most recently, against  Brazil's court system (STJ).
 RansomEXX is what security researchers call a " big-game hunter" or " human-operated ransomware." These two terms are used to describe ransomware groups that hunt large targets in search for big paydays, knowing that some companies or government agencies can't afford to stay down while they recover their systems.
 These groups buy access or breach networks themselves, expand access to as many systems as possible, and then manually deploy their ransomware binary as a final payload to cripple as much of the target's infrastructure as possible.
 But over the past year, there has been a paradigm shift into how these groups operate.
 Many ransomware gangs have realized that attacking workstations first isn't a lucrative deal, as companies will tend to re-image affected systems and move on without paying ransoms.
   In recent months, in many incidents, some ransomware gangs haven't bothered encrypting workstations, and have first and foremost, targeted crucial servers inside a company's network, knowing that by taking down these systems first, companies wouldn't be able to access their centralized data troves, even if workstations were unaffected.
 The RansomEXX gang creating a Linux version of their Windows ransomware is in tune with how many companies operate today, with many firms running internal systems on Linux, and not always on Windows Server.
 A Linux version makes perfect sense from an attacker's perspective; always looking to expand and touch as much core infrastructure as possible in their quest to cripple companies and demand higher ransoms.
 What we see from RansomEXX may soon turn out to be an industry-defining trend, with other big ransomware groups rolling out their Linux versions in the future as well.
 And, this trend appears to have already begun. According to cyber-security firm Emsisoft, besides RansomEXX, the  Mespinoza (Pysa) ransomware gang has also recently developed a Linux variant from their initial Windows version.
 But Linux ransomware is also not unique. In the past years, other ransomware gangs have created Linux ransomware strains as well, such as the Snatch group. However, those groups were small-time operations that relied on spam campaigns to infect victims, were rarely successful, and did not engage in targeted intrusions like the current generation of ransomware groups we see today.
 Emsisoft says the RansomEXX Linux variants they've detected were seen as far back as July. Configuring systems to detect RansomEXX Linux variants isn't a solid strategy because of the way big-game hunter ransomware crews operate. By the time attackers deploy the ransomware, they already own most of a company's network. The best strategy companies can take against these types of intrusions is to secure network perimeters by applying security patches to gateway devices and by making sure they are not misconfigured with weak or default credentials.
title: 网络攻击者现在还制作他们的勒索软件的Linux版本
title_en: Cyberattackers Now Also Make Linux Versions of Their Ransomware

note:
Facebook已经开始对一些群体进行缓刑，这是其减缓虚假信息传播的最新举措，并试图破坏美国大选的合法性。
如果该公司发现有太多违反其社区标准的帖子，无论是公共的还是私人的，都将被迫让管理员和版主手动审批每一条提交的帖子。这一要求将对该组织有效60天，无法上诉或推翻。
该公司将密切关注群组管理员和版主在这两个月内如何处理帖子，如果一个群组一再允许太多冒犯性帖子，该公司可能会决定完全关闭该群组。这一变化使得管理团队的志愿者对团队内部发生的事情负有更多责任。
脸书公司发言人伦纳德·林说：“我们暂时要求美国一些政治和社会团体的管理员和版主批准所有帖子，如果他们的团体有成员违反社区标准的话。”他说，该公司采取这一措施“是为了在这一前所未有的时刻保护人们”。
在新的限制措施出台之前，该公司本周采取了其他措施，以遏制阴谋论和针对即将到来的选举结果的暴力呼声的病毒式传播。大选前，Facebook宣布将在投票结束后暂时停止政治广告，并设计了一个标签，以防总统候选人过早宣布获胜。本周早些时候，该公司将这个标签添加到特朗普的帖子中。
自那以后，该公司一直在尝试新的临时策略，以跟上虚假信息和阴谋论的激增。例如，Facebook表示，这将使找到与破坏计票合法性有关的术语变得更加困难，并减少与选举相关的直播视频的分发。
本周，特朗普总统的盟友利用Facebook页面和群组散布毫无根据的阴谋论，称民主党人试图“窃取”民主党提名人乔·拜登(Joe Biden)的选举。脸书星期四采取行动，清除了推动面对面抗议活动的最大组织之一“阻止偷窃”，该组织有36万名成员。Facebook表示，由于“令人担忧的暴力呼声”和试图使选举过程合法化，该组织已被移除。
Facebook还没有公开宣布针对群体的更严格的政策，目前还不清楚它是否正在部署或测试尚未公开的类似措施。
首批被列入Facebook观察名单的群体中，有些人措手不及。华盛顿州阿伯丁市一个颇受欢迎的公共组织的管理员发现，他们必须通过Facebook通知批准每一篇新帖子，并立即生效。它说，所有帖子“现在都需要在1月4日之前批准。”该组织主要讨论当地的事件、商业和问题，拥有7000多名成员，并制定了一项反对争论政治或煽动其他成员的政策。
当地平面设计师黛布·布莱查(Deb Blecha)说，“在这一点上，我们想知道是否可以关闭这个团体。”她在这个团体担任管理员已有10年之久。“额外的工作和挫折感给我们带来了沉重的负担。”
Facebook和其他社交媒体公司长期以来一直依赖无偿的群管理员来处理群中帖子的大部分审核工作。在Facebook的案例中，它使用人工智能和专业内容版主相结合的方式来发现极具问题的内容，但更微妙的决定留给了志愿者。它们是警察的争论，是抵御错误信息的第一道防线，有了这项新措施，他们要对他们允许的那种对话负有密切责任。
note_en:
Facebook has started putting some groups on a type of probation, its latest move to slow the spread of disinformation and attempts to undermine the legitimacy of the U.S. election.
 Any group, public or private, the company detects has too many posts that violate its community standards will be forced to have administrators and moderators approve each submission manually. The requirement will stay in place for 60 days for the group, with no way to appeal or override it.
 The company will be closely monitoring how group administrators and moderators handle posts during those two months, and could decide to shut a group down completely if it repeatedly allows too many offending posts. The change makes the volunteers who run groups more responsible for what happens inside them.
  “We are temporarily requiring admins and moderators of some political and social groups in the U.S. to approve all posts, if their group has a number of Community Standards violations from members,” said Facebook company spokesperson Leonard Lam. He said the company was taking the measure “in order to protect people during this unprecedented time.”
  The new limitation follows other measures the company enacted this week to curb the viral spread of conspiracy theories and calls to violence over pending election results. Ahead of the election, Facebook announced it would temporarily stop political ads after polls closed, and it devised a label to use in case a  presidential candidate prematurely claimed victory. It added that label to Trump posts earlier this week.
  Since then, the company has been trying out new — temporary — tactics to keep up with a surge in disinformation and conspiracy theories. For example, Facebook said it would make it harder to find terms related to undermining the legitimacy of ballot counts, and reduce the distribution of election-related live videos.
  Allies of President Trump have used Facebook pages and groups this week to spread a baseless conspiracy theory that Democrats are attempting to “steal” the election for Democratic nominee Joe Biden. Facebook took action Thursday, removing one of the largest groups pushing for in-person protests called “STOP THE STEAL,” which had 360,000 members. Facebook said it removed the group because of “worrying calls for violence” and attempts to delegitimize the election process.
 Facebook had not publicly announced the stricter policies for groups, and it was unclear whether it is deploying or testing similar measures that are not yet public.
  Some of the first groups to be put on Facebook’s watch list were caught off guard. Admins of a popular public group for the city of Aberdeen, Wash., found out they would have to approve each new post, effective immediately, via a Facebook notification. It said all posts “now require approval until Jan. 4.” The group, which mostly discusses local events, business and issues, has more than 7,000 members and a policy against arguing about politics or inciting other members.
   “At this point we are wondering if we might close the group,” said Deb Blecha, a local graphic designer who has been an admin on the group for 10 years. “The extra work and the frustration is weighing heavy on us.”
 Facebook and other social media companies have long relied on unpaid group administrators to handle the bulk of moderation for posts in their groups. In Facebook’s case, it uses a combination of artificial intelligence and professional content moderators to find extremely problematic content, but more nuanced decisions are left to volunteers. They police arguments, are a first line of defense against misinformation, and with this new measure, are held closely responsible for the kinds of conversations they allow.
title: Facebook开始对一些政治和社会团体进行60天的试用期，并将密切监控他们，以减缓有关选举的虚假信息的传播
title_en: Facebook starts putting some political and social groups on a 60 day probation and will closely monitor them, to slow the spread of disinfo about the election

note:
七个太阳的不合时宜的衰落导致了数千年的忽视，逐渐将大型机庞大的代码库转变为一场半感官、半确定性子例程的竞赛。它们存在于一种不断变化的状态中，就好像被它们的编程困在了一种扭曲的数字炼狱中。
你，程序，必须决定是帮助还是阻碍主机的居民。这里的居民奇特而多样，许多人被发现天生就怀有敌意。一整套强大的武器和能力可以帮助你生存、防御和摧毁。
note_en:
The untimely fall of the Seven Suns lead to a millenia of neglect, gradually transforming the Mainframe’s vast repository of code into a race of half-sentient, half-deterministic subroutines. They exist in a state of flux, as if trapped by their programming in a kind of twisted digital purgatory.
 You, The Program, must decide on whether to help or hinder the denizens of the Mainframe. The inhabitants are strange and diverse, and many are found to be inherently hostile. A full complement of powerful weapons and abilities are available to help you survive, defend and destroy.

title: 奎恩继电器

note:
观景廊-11张图片一座微小到肉眼看不见的雕塑被称为有史以来最小的人形雕塑。艺术家乔蒂·赫尔维茨(Jonty Hurwitz)的微型人体雕像尺寸为20 x 80 x 100微米，是一系列同样小巧的新雕塑的一部分。这些雕塑的尺寸极其微小，每个雕塑的大小都与你的指甲在大约6小时内生长的数量大致相同，只能用扫描电子显微镜才能看到。
这些艺术品采用先进的纳米3D打印技术，结合多光子光刻技术，使用双光子吸收现象的激光创作而成。通过这种方式，用激光在一块感光单体或聚合物凝胶中追踪出一个物体，然后把多余的部分洗掉，留下固体的形状。
由于这种双光子吸收的方法只发生在激光的微小焦点上，因此它实质上是在那个结合点创建了一个微小的3D像素(体素)。然后，激光在计算机控制下沿分数距离移动，并形成该系列中的下一个体素。在长达数小时的艰苦过程中，完整的3D雕塑是一个接一个的体素组装而成的。
赫维茨说：我们生活在一个不可能的事情终于成为现实的时代。在我们自己小小的方式中，我们已经变成了半个创造之神。以我的拙见，当代艺术需要反映今天的人类状况，它需要代表创作时的社会状态。花一点时间想想，就在6000年前，我们还在用岩石在洞穴的墙上画粗糙的动物形象。我们已经走了很远。这座纳米雕塑是全人类的集体成就。它是几千年研发的巅峰之作。
这位艺术家说，赫维茨创作的《丘比特与普赛克：初吻》(The First Kiss)、《信任》(Trust)和《强度》(Intensire)等雕塑都是为了探索科学与传说、神话和现实之间的关系。
绝对的事实是：肉眼看不到这些雕塑，他继续说道。你看到的只是一面小镜子，上面有……。上面什么都没有。只有在强大的扫描电子显微镜的屏幕上才能看到这些作品。那么你怎么知道这座雕塑真的存在呢？参与其中的唯一方式是通过屏幕和鼠标，通过真空和一系列数学上令人兴奋的量子过程将你和艺术品分开，这些量子过程向艺术品喷洒粒子以绘制其轮廓。如果你的基本感官告诉你什么都没有，你能确信它的存在吗？神话和科学之间的界线是正确的。
下面的视频是对这位艺术家的采访，他在视频中解释了更多关于他的作品。

note_en:
View gallery - 11 images   A sculpture so tiny that it cannot be seen by the naked eye is claimed to be the smallest sculpture of the human form ever created. Measuring a picayune 20 x 80 x 100 microns, artist Jonty Hurwitz’s tiny human statue is part of a new series of equally diminutive new sculptures that are at a scale so infinitesimally miniscule that each of the figures is approximately equal in size to the amount your fingernails grow in around about 6 hours, and can only be viewed using a scanning electron microscope.
 Sculpted with an advanced new nano 3D printing technology coupled with a technique called  multiphoton lithography, these works of art are created using a laser that uses the phenomenon of  two photon absorption. In this way, an object is traced out by a laser in a block of light-sensitive monomer or polymer gel, and the excess is then washed away to leave a solid form.
 As this method of two photon absorption only takes place at the tiny focal point of the laser, it essentially creates a tiny 3D pixel (a  voxel) at that juncture. The laser is then moved along a fractional distance under computer control and the next voxel in the series is formed. In a long and painstaking process that takes place over many hours, the complete 3D sculpture is assembled voxel by voxel.
 "We live in an era where the impossible has finally come to pass," said Hurwitz. "In our own little way we have become demi-gods of creation. Contemporary art, in my humble view, needs to reflect the human condition as it is today, it needs to represent the state of society at the time of its creation. Take a moment to consider that only 6,000 years ago we were painting crude animal images on the walls of caves with rocks. We have come far. This nano sculpture is the collective achievement of all of humanity. It is the culmination of thousands of years of R&D.;"
   The sculptures that Hurwitz created, "Trust," "Cupid and Psyche: The First Kiss," and "Intensity" are said by the artist to explore the idea of science versus legend, myth, and reality.
 "The absolute fact is this: the human eye is unable to see these sculptures," he continued. "All you see is a small mirror with ... nothing on it. The only way to perceive these works is on the screen of powerful scanning electron microscope. So how can you ever know that this sculpture really exists? Your only way to engage with it is through a screen, and a mouse separating you and the art via a vacuum and a series of mathematically mind-blowing quantum processes that shower the art with particles to map its contours. Can you be sure of its existence if your basic senses are telling you that nothing is there? The line between myth and science is fine."
 The video below is an interview with the artist in which he explains more about his work.
